title: Housing plan ‘hurts’, not ‘helps’
full_text:
Labor’s expansion of the 5 per cent deposit scheme, coupled with its tax changes, opens up taxpayers to more risk and could hurt the very people the government intends to help, the insurance industry has declared.
With the housing market at its weakest in years, the Insurance Council has told the Senate Labor’s expanded 5 per cent deposit scheme is “not achieving its policy goals” and underwrites a “transfer of wealth” from young Australians to established investors.
Instead of helping the lower-income, prospective first-home buyers Labor said it would aid, the expansion of the scheme had instead given “some of the greatest benefits” to people who sold properties eligible for the scheme due to inflation introduced by the policy.
“These households, including investors, upgraders, and other households the scheme is not intended to support, are among the biggest winners, with the government effectively underwriting a transfer of wealth to them,” Insurance Council executive director Andrew Hall wrote in his submission to the parliamentary inquiry on productivity. “We believe the scheme weakens competitive neutrality … productivity is supported when private markets can allocate capital, price risk and innovate efficiently.” The intervention comes ahead of Anthony Albanese and Jim Chalmers heading into the ALP national conference next week cementing their tax changes into Labor’s platform.
The Insurance Council warns the budget could hamper the government’s fiscal firepower during crises “precisely when it is needed most”.
Fresh figures from Cotality showed the auction clearance rate had been below 50 per cent for each of the past seven weeks.
The Albanese government last October significantly expanded the 5 per cent first-home buyer deposit scheme. The scheme allows first-home buyers to take out mortgages with just 5 per cent of a home’s purchase value instead of the usual 20 per cent, with the government acting as the guarantor. The government raised the price ceiling on properties that could be purchased under the scheme and removed the cap on the number of people the program could support, fulfilling an election pledge.
The Insurance Council said its members reported this had a “deleterious impact” on the lenders mortgage insurance industry “as a major part of its premium base in first home buyers is drawn instead to a free government competitor”.
The Albanese government portrayed this as a selling point when it announced the policy.
“For the average first-home buyer, this cuts the time to save for a deposit and saves tens of thousands of dollars on lenders mortgage insurance,” Anthony Albanese and Housing Minister Clare O’Neil said at the time.
The Insurance Council comments add to a growing chorus warning about the Labor government’s housing agenda.
The Insurance Council said that on top of the people who had seen “some of the greatest benefits” selling into the expanded 5 per cent deposit boom, the softening housing market meant some of the people who had bought into that inflated market now faced the prospect of negative equity. “The outcome of this combination is increased mortgage stress,” Mr Hall wrote.
“Mortgage stress requires tough household spending decisions, leading to fewer savings and reduced economic resilience. The scheme is likely distorting household borrowing decisions.
“By reducing the price signal associated with credit risk, the scheme may encourage households to assume higher leverage than they otherwise would … for these borrowers, the effects can be prolonged, constraining future consumption and labour mobility.” If these people defaulted on their mortgages because of an economic downturn, the government would have to realise these losses and this would be “socialised among all taxpayers”, it said.
“In the event of an economic downturn or a recession, there would be broader and increased calls on government spending as reliance on social welfare rises,” the council added.
“The risk is that the government’s capacity to boost productivity and stimulate economic growth is diminished precisely when it is needed most.” When contacted for comment, Ms O’Neil’s office defended the government’s policies.
“We make no apologies for helping hundreds of thousands of first home buyers get into their own home while saving them $2.3bn in lenders mortgage insurance costs,” a spokesman said.
“Without this scheme, many first-home buyers wouldn’t be able to enter the market at all.” EDITORIAL P10
